turns.text: -- the regulation is a valid regulation despite the Government's present position, then would not the proper disposition be to send this case back for determination as to the dispute between, namely, whether it's actual horsepower or nominal horsepower that the regulation refers to.

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: No, we -- we would say no because I can't conceive of the finding that the ruling is proper without also determining whether or not the horsepower is determined in terms of actual horsepower or nominal horsepower if --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well, we can't determine that.

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: Well, that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: That's for the lower court to determine that issue. It was never determined by the Court of Appeals because they said horsepower was an irrelevancy and that the regulation was invalid.

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: Well, we are here to sustain the view of the Court of Appeals.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well, I understand that.

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: I mean that is an alternative position here. One position is that the ruling, if construed in terms of nominal horsepower, imposes the tax and even though that test may too narrowly restrict the statute, that -- that would not be an issue here because it does apply to these units. On the other hand, if it is an actual horsepower test, it is clearly invalid because these units which are taxable by every conceivable criterion would be exempt from the tax. So we say there is no basis upon which to remand it to the District Court here. These units are taxable under the statute by any test that can be conceived of. The horsepower test is not rationally consistent with the statute. Therefore, the Court of Appeals was correct in reversing the District Court in holding that the units were taxable. That's our basic position.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Heffron, in the trial court, you stipulated that the -- or the Government stipulated that these units were of the household type.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Now, may I ask you this question? If it had not been for that stipulation, would there been -- have been any issues open to counsel or to the taxpayer that are not open to him in the case with the stipulation?

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: No, precisely the same issues would be involved. The taxpayer would still be contending that under the ruling, however -- whatever the statute says, under the ruling, and this is his sole contention, "Your ruling exempts my units from tax." Now, there's nothing in the ruling which refers in terms to household type. There is nothing in the statute. It has always been open for -- to the taxpayer to say, "My unit is not self-contained for reasons A, B and C. All of my unit is not household type for reasons D, E and F." He has never said that. He has said only one thing and one thing only at every stage and that is “Because of the horsepower test in your rulings, my units are exempted." Now, if this -- if this Court should hold the statute, it only applies to self-contained units, there would be no further issues to litigate here.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, let me ask you this now. Excuse me.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Go -- go finish.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I understand his contention that you are estop and I understand your contention that in someway we are estop by you having agreed that this 50,000 would be the same as the two. Now -- however, there's a provision with reference to appeal that we construed in (Inaudible) which called attention to the fact that the statute says that we dispose of the case as justice requires. Now, let us assume that we hold the statute is -- is valid under your claim, not under his, that would mean, of course, that he did pass on the tax in the two and he would not be entitled to have that retried anymore to but why do we have to say that with the situation changing as it has, they shouldn't have a chance to have the case reopened for that to be tried out in the court below if -- whether he passed it on or not, except by -- should you stipulate it so that if justice required it, we shouldn't do that?

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: In your stipulation -- does your stipulation -- is your stipulation a stipulation that bars them from saying as to the none two cases, the other 50,000, they didn't pass it on? I don't think your stipulation does that. It merely says that this is a stipulation as to the scope and the efficacy of the -- of the ruling of the Commission, is that right?

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: Yes. Well, the taxpayer can no longer file a claim -- no, I beg your pardon. I'm going to say, if the stipulation means anything here, the only reason the Commissioner refrained from collecting the tax on these units was because of a stipulation that this was a test case and the disposition of the tax with respect to these --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: On the merits --

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: -- two units would control the others.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- of the legality of the tax. On the merits of the legality of the tax not to be passed it on and therefore, he's not out of pocket.

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: I said with regard to the rights of the plaintiffs to obtain a refund here and they would be limited again by the pleading -- by the claim for refund which is the basic document which they drafted to shape the issues in this litigation.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: In this case, but would they as to the 50,000?

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: Yes, we say they would.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Why should the Government want a holding for these 50,000 units on this so-called ambiguous stipulation? If you conceive that under the law, he may have a right to get refund because he has not passed them on, why should the Government take that position?

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: Well, we don't conceive that under the -- under any view of the law, they have a right to refund here.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I -- I understand that. Well, I understand you do conceive that in a test over this that if he has not passed this tax on, he would have a right to show that under the section to which you referred in order to get a refund.

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: I say -- we -- we say he had a right to show that and did not avail himself of that opportunity.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: That he had a right -- had a right below but you want to hold him on the 50,000 on the basis of the two.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Are you saying, Mr. Heffron, we could not authorize him to amend that claim of refund if that's so important in order to set up the facts which would bring him within the right to a statutory relief?

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: No. I'm not -- I'm not suggesting that.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: He could do that, couldn't he, as to the 50,000 units?

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: I suppose this Court could direct me.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: But we do say that as this litigation was tried, the taxpayer never raised that claim. He never raised it. That would be tantamount to a taxpayer suing and saying, "The reason why I'm entitled to refund is because the transaction is not taxable for X.” Having gone up to this Court and losing, he is given an opportunity to go back to the District Court and say, "Well, I have another reason which I never mentioned before as to why these units are not taxable."

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: What problem is that if that's -- if he's entitled to something under the law that Congress has passed?

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: Well, we say that it is so clear that these units are --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, I doubt this test, [Laughs] you would take it the law is clear and so forth.[Laughter]

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: Well -- well --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But the -- also, the law is clear, isn't it, I understood you from that statute as a man who pays the tax, an excise tax and he's relied on a ruling of the remedy which turns out to be bad, the Congress is seen fit to give him a special relief under the section to which you refer us. Now, if that's true in this case, why should the Government insist that he'd be cutoff from that because of that test?

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: Well, because that -- that issue came into this case simply by way of the Government's attempting to show this Court that there was no collection of the tax on the theory that if there was such -- that if the taxpayer has failed to collect it, they would have asserted it and won their case based on this estoppel ground.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But isn't the --

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: But they never asked for the benefit of this statute.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But isn't a fact that they were -- isn't a fact that the bone of contention in this controversy was the taxability of these units, is that right? That's what the litigation was about.

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: The -- the right --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: If this litigation was the Government's claim to have a right to tax and their claim that, as a matter of statutory construction and regulation, you did not have a right to tax.

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: There --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Wasn't that the issue?

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And therefore, the question, his collateral question, each side was so -- he was as -- he was as confident on his reading of the law as you are of yours and the question whether he's entitled to get a refund because he would be out of pocket wasn't in -- perhaps in the conscious contemplation of the parties.

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: Well, we -- we say that it -- that it was. But in any event, we say that a position which would grant him --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: How do you know it was? You're saying it was because he didn't raise that as a claim.

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: Well, this was raised below. In the District Court, he had an opportunity. The Government counsel, in his closing remarks to the District Judge, said, "Why hasn't he alleged that he failed to collect the tax? We've conceded that this ruling is ambiguous. Why hasn't he alleged it to you, District Judge? Why doesn't he make that claim now?" And the response was, and this is in the record and the Court can read it there, the response was, "There is nothing in the record about what we did about this tax and that's not in the case." And that is why the taxpayer --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's exactly why he wanted to get a ruling for the future.

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: Because --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: They didn't merely wanted to be put hold for a stuff that -- money they spent, they wanted to get a ruling for the future.

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: As a matter of fact, the Court will see from the record that this taxpayer stated below, "There is no issue of the estoppel. We will not prevent you from arguing that your ruling -- that the ruling, if construed, to exempt us from tax is invalid." He stated that below. He has never sought the benefit of this provision. As a matter of fact, he repudiated the suggestion that he produced evidence to show that he, in fact, failed to collect the tax from his vendees. The closing remarks --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) full time --

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: This --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- without either of you arguing the real merit of the case that is before us, namely, what's the scope of the statute.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Very well. Mr. Rothschild, I'm -- I want to ask you a question. I don't want you to reargue your case.

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: I won't, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: You took your time, but I want to ask you the case because I want to know before we adjourn. And I'm speaking now to these 50,000 units.

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Did you pass a tax on to your -- to your purchasers and collected on the theory that you are going to pay it to the Government and now seek to -- to deprive the Government of the right to collect that tax and to keep that money or did you, relying upon the regulations and rulings of the Department, failed to pass that on to your -- to your purchasers in which event you would now owe the Government a million dollars or so out of your own pocket? Which of the two is correct?

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: The latter, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: The latter is true?

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: Yes. Now, I know -- I know you don't want to reargue. May I ask --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: -- may I state just one thing?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes, yes, you may answer that question rather boldly.

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: That's right. In connection with that question on why it is not in the record, is that proper?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes, but that is your answer that you would now be stuck for a million or so dollars --

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- that you have not passed on to your customers.

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: And that is the -- that is the point.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And as -- as to this two -- the two -- the inferences in the litigation, what is that fact there that you (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: On these two units? We certainly did as I explained to, I think was Justice Whittaker. These were units that happened to have been sold to -- directly to a lawyer and to an accountant of the taxpayer before it came to our attention that -- that these units were exempt under the rulings because they had been mislabeled. We simply selected these two units which had a $29.93 was paid to the Government without a protest passed on to the consumer and collected from him on these units. And it was after we decided to bring a test suit that we happened to pick these two units, and it was after that point that we sold the $50,000 units, and we brought these suits on these two test units simply to test the taxability of the 50,000. And there is no conceivable procedural way in which we could've put on any evidence such as Mr. Heffron says, "We should have put on to prove that we had suffered a loss in the 50,000 without going through 50,000 claims for refund in -- in the same manner."

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Tell me this. Is it -- is it strange that I should think that it is strange that you should stipulate that 50 -- as to 50,000 cases, they should be governed by these two instances as a test suit when there's such a cardinal difference between the two?

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: No, because the only -- the only similarity, Your Honor, and the only reason that -- that we stipulate it was because these suits in these air conditioners, the 50,000 in physical content, in size, in horsepower were the same as the two and --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But -- but there was all the difference in the world and in the one case, you wouldn't be out of pocket and in the other cases, you would be.

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: Well, we'd be out of pocket. In a sense, we pay back $29.93 but that --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, I know but -- but --

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: But --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- if a case is a test case, then presumably it has the same ingredients as those for which it is a test case.

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: Well, it's a test case for one purpose, Your Honor. It was a test case to determine the applicability of the excise tax to units of the size described in the stipulation of facts.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But suppose you were wrong in your law, you could still recover for the 50,000.

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: If we're wrong in our law, Your Honor, and we are -- and here's -- here's what will happen. If we're wrong and the Government wins this case, then we will have to pay by over a million dollars of taxes, file claims for refund, hope that we can persuade the Treasury, which I don't think we will, that we should -- that they shouldn't charge us this tax, then we would have to file tests -- additional suits and make affirmative proof that we -- that we absorbed the tax on all of these 50,000 units.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But you just said to the Chief Justice that you haven't -- that you didn't (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: Well, I've got to prove that in 50,000 cases.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: All I'm saying is if it does strike (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: I mean it's not in the record. Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: It just strikes me. It's a little odd that you didn't need yourself an act of the (Inaudible) so important to the matter.

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: Well, that's the only act that we have, Your Honor. We -- we made a test suit to determine the applicability of the tax.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- based out a very important basis on which you could recover a million dollars.

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: No, we couldn't possible recover it on that basis --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Now, Mr. --

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: -- on this procedure.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- Rothschild, may I ask you one -- just one more --

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- more question? You complained of the shift in the Government's position on the stipulation that they made concerning the household type of --

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: That is right.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- of these units. Will you answer me this question if you can, please?

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: If that -- if that stipulation had been -- not been made by the Government --

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- would there have been any issues available to you in this lawsuit that were -- that were actually foreclosed by reason of this stipulation?

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: Well, certainly, Your Honor. Household type, whether or not these air conditioners were primarily designed for -- to meet the needs of a household was a question of fact which was not tried because of the Government's stipulation. The other question is whether they are self-contained which is the new issue raised by the Government on its brief on the merits. Self-contained is now a word of art because the Government has actually an exhibit saying that it covers anything from a small portable to 1000 horsepower plant. And the only expression of the industry as to the meaning of self-contained, and that's urged by the Government's issues of fact, the only expression of the industry takes in horsepower and the Government itself says horsepower applies so that if self-contained -- so that if these units are just self-contained as a matter of law as the Government contains now, why would they be relying as they do in their brief, Your Honor, on the horsepower test on the understanding of the industry and on the intention of the Commissioner? Those are issues of fact which were not raised in the trial court which are raised in this Court and which we have had no opportunity to meet.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: That's true on both.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Let me ask you one question --

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- about that issue of fact. I thought it was conceded that these whole household room units.

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: They are room units.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, why -- why do you say there'd be a question as whether or not they were household appliance?

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: Because the larger -- and this -- the evidence shows this, Your Honor, in the record. The larger units are considered commercial units.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: There is an arbitrary line --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I'm not talking about the horsepower question. I'm just talking about whether they were households.

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: Well, that's -- that's the principal distinction between household and commercial is size. That's been carried --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Do you mean when it get above -- you -- when it get above one ton, they are not household?

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: When you get up to one or one and a half, you will find their primary use is -- is not in house -- and that was certainly true when the regulation was passed in 1948, the -- there's consumption.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Now, I want to ask you one other question.

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Curiosity maybe.

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Doesn't that tax go up on June the 1st?

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: No, Your Honor, unless I'm wrong.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: On the household units? It's been advertised that it's going up on June the1st in all the places. And they told me it was going up June the 1st, it'd be by now.[Laughter] Do you say it's not going up? Did -- did Congress not increase it right -- recently?

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: Oh, no, I think it's still 10%. I would refer to the Government. I don't know. Is there any evidence?

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: That means this case is done.

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: That reference is probably to the applicability of the new regulations.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Oh, the new regulations. That's what --

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- is being talked about.

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: All would impose the tax on units which had (Inaudible) not been taxable under the rulings.

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: Well, yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: On June the 1st.

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: Well, that -- that must be --

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: Oh, that is in effect already but it only applies to units which were sold by the manufacturer subsequent to a given date.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Subsequent to the time this --

turns.name: HOWARD A. HEFFRON
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: This new regulation --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- ruling (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: -- that came out. And the new regulation, by the way, also creates an administrative definition of self-contained as Your Honors will know.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. --

turns.name: EDWIN A. ROTHSCHILD
turns.text: Thank you. Thank you gentlemen.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- Mr. Rothschild.[Laughter]
